Mindset Adjustments When Learning Something New

Helping out a few friends with their learning blockers lately, I kept noticing the same patterns popping up — patterns I'd hit myself early on. So I figured I'd write down what I learned the hard way.
Apologies in advance for the messy writing ((shrinks back
Don't assume you already get it
A lot of the time we assume we get it, and skip past the foundational stuff we should actually know.

A lot of people skip foundational courses when learning new tech. Take me — I used to do PHP & MySQL. After I got out of the military, I treated the foundational PHP & MySQL courses like they didn't exist, and went straight to signing up for a Laravel course. Result: I couldn't follow along at all. Everything sounded like wizardry, and I burned NT$4,000.

Then my first job needed C#.MVC, so I went looking for a hands-on C#.MVC course — and again, skipped the C# basics, thinking my PHP background would carry me. Same outcome: I couldn't follow. The real problem was that I had zero C# foundation. Another NT$2,400 down the drain.

I bet some people are thinking: "Wait, I did PHP & MySQL — why can't I just pick up C#.MVC? Aren't all languages roughly the same?" Yeah, they are kind of similar. But the reason was simple — I never went deep into the language-level mechanics, the native APIs, or built up real fundamentals. I just kept looking for shortcuts.

So when you're picking a course, audit a session and check if you actually understand the basics first. Once you do, then go for the advanced track. Don't dive into advanced material blind — you'll just waste your time and your poor wallet.
A lot of things are built on top of fundamentals — same as building a house.
It took me a while to recognize this one. Before I did, my brain was full of these: "I already know this stuff", "Yeah I get it", "Why would I waste time on this foundations course?"

Those exact thoughts kept biting me at work, because I could only use libraries or apply pre-built templates. The second I needed to customize something or maintain anything, I was stuck — let alone fixing bugs.

Take Bootstrap, which is in my main wheelhouse (frontend). Early on, I treated Bootstrap as a templating tool. Want me to slap together a webpage from a template? No problem. Want me to customize the Bootstrap styles? I'd freeze, because I never went deep on Bootstrap's native CSS underneath. I was a use-it-don't-touch-it engineer.

Early on I also touched WordPress, Moodle, Joomla!, OpenCart, etc. I could spin up a site fast. But customize them? Nope — because I never went into the underlying PHP layer or studied how those CMSes were actually built.
If at all possible, build your foundation properly. Problems become easier to read, and you don't max out your wall-collision skill tree.
Don't get trapped by past experience
Don't take something you already learned and force-fit that mental model onto the new thing.
This one I see a lot in friends. Past experience can absolutely help you ramp up faster on new tech, but sometimes it actively gets in your way.

Take my own example — I mentioned my first job needed C#.MVC. At the time I thought C#.MVC was super annoying because in PHP I'd been writing in whatever style I felt like, anywhere I wanted. So I felt the C#.MVC dev pattern was tedious and overcomplicated. Result: when I was trying to understand MVC, there was this weird wall in my head that kept blocking me, and it took me six months to actually click on it.
So when you're learning something new, try to keep a beginner's mindset. Don't let some past experience turn into your own wall.
Don't be the "just use the library" person
When you reach for a third-party lib or feature, try to also understand the underlying tech.

This is basically a sibling of "don't skip the fundamentals". Back when I built sites, I'd just google for a ready-made library. So whenever I needed to modify or use those libs, I'd burn extra time — all because my foundation was thin.
After I went deep on frontend, if a feature had time pressure, I'd think about whether I could build it myself within the deadline. If yes, I'd take a swing at it. If not, I'd grab a library.
What if there's no time pressure? Then I'd study someone else's library and try to build it from scratch myself.

Long story short: when you're learning new tech, hold to the four "don'ts" — don't assume you get it, don't skip the fundamentals, don't get trapped by past experience, don't be the use-the-library person. You'll save yourself a lot of wall-hitting.
